step.title: Step 75
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT then DOWN around white strip')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 130, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Moving DOWN changes structure color from c to G. Left structure split: top (G, rows 28-31) and bottom (c, rows 32-35). Player at rows 24-26, cols 16-19.
- Plan: Continue DOWN to convert more of the left structure.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 129 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player didn't move DOWN - still at rows 24-26, cols 16-19. The structure didn't change either. It seems like the player can't move further DOWN because there's a white strip at row 27 blocking the way, or the player is blocked by the structure below.
Wait, looking more carefully: the player is at rows 24-26 and the white strip is at row 27. The structure starts at row 28. So the player is 1 row above the white strip. When I tried DOWN, the player moved from rows 24-27 to rows 24-26 (shrinking vertically?). No, the player was at rows 24-27 before and now is at rows 24-26. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the previous state:
- Before DOWN: N pixels=12 rows=[24-27] cols=[16-19] (4 rows tall)
- After first DOWN: N pixels=12 rows=[24-26] cols=[16-19] (3 rows tall)
Wait, that doesn't make sense for a 3x4 block. Let me reconsider. The player is 3 rows x 4 cols = 12 pixels. Before the first DOWN action in this sequence, the player was at rows 24-27 (4 rows). But 4 rows x 4 cols = 16 pixels, not 12. So maybe the player was actually at rows 24-26 (3 rows) and the white strip was at row 27.
Let me re-read the output from the DOWN action:
- id=1 color=N pixels=12 rows=[24-26] cols=[16-19]
- id=2 color=W pixels=4 rows=[27-27] cols=[16-19]
So the player is 3 rows (24-26) x 4 cols (16-19) = 12 pixels. The white strip is 1 row (27) x 4 cols = 4 pixels. The structure starts at row 28.
When I tried DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, the player didn't move. This means the player is blocked from moving DOWN - possibly by the white strip or by the structure.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe the player needs to be INSIDE the structure to change its color. Let me check if the player can move into the structure. But the white strip at row 27 might be blocking.
Wait, looking at the initial state again: the player started at rows 49-51, cols 32-35 with a white strip at row 48. The structure at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 was above the white strip. So the initial layout was: structure (rows 36-39), white strip (row 48), player (rows 49-51).
The player was BELOW the structure initially, not above it. So maybe the player needs to move UP to interact with structures?
But currently the player is at rows 24-26, which is ABOVE the structures (rows 28+). The white strip is at row 27, between the player and the structure.
Let me try UP to move away from the structure, then see if I can move to a different column and then DOWN into a structure from the side.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The first DOWN action DID change the structure - it changed from c to G for the top part. But subsequent DOWNs didn't work because the player is blocked.
Let me try moving LEFT or RIGHT to get around the white strip and then DOWN into the structure from the side.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player blocked from moving DOWN by white strip at row 27. First DOWN converted top of left structure from c to G. Need to find another way to interact.
Plan: Try moving LEFT to get around the white strip, then DOWN into the structure from the side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try LEFT then DOWN
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT then DOWN around white strip')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows=[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}] cols=[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Nodes:
  id=1 color=W pixels=4 rows=[24-27] cols=[12-12]
  id=2 color=N pixels=12 rows=[24-27] cols=[13-15]
  id=3 color=c pixels=24 rows=[28-35] cols=[16-19]
  id=4 color=c pixels=12 rows=[28-31] cols=[24-27]
  id=5 color=b pixels=28 rows=[28-31] cols=[28-39]
  id=6 color=b pixels=4 rows=[29-30] cols=[17-18]
  id=7 color=b pixels=4 rows=[29-30] cols=[25-26]
  id=8 color=g pixels=20 rows=[29-30] cols=[29-38]
  id=9 color=b pixels=4 rows=[33-34] cols=[17-18]
  id=10 color=P pixels=22 rows=[63-63] cols=[0-21]
  id=11 color=c pixels=42 rows=[63-63] cols=[22-63]
